title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 5 — Cao Cao Forges a Decree to Rally Lords Against Dong Zhuo / The Three Brothers Battle Lü Bu at Hulao Pass
text:
At the close of the last chapter,Chen Gongwas about to slayCao Cao. Yet he mused, “It was for the sake of the state that I came with him; to kill him would be unjust. Better to leave him here and go my own way.”

He put away his sword and mounted his horse and rode away before dawn, heading back to his home inDong commandery.

WhenCao Caowoke up and saw no sign ofChen Gong, he thought to himself, “The man thinks I am wicked because of a couple of egoistic phrases I used, so he has left me behind. I ought to push on too and not lingerhere.”•••

The story ofCao CaoandChen Gong’s bloody flight and separation became a Peking opera “The Capture and Liberation of Cao”

Cao Caoreached his home atChenliu commanderythat night. When he saw his father, he related all that had happened and said he wanted to distribute the family wealth in order to attract and recruit soldiers for an uprising.

“I fear our possessions are but small,” said his father, “and not enough to do anything with. Yet one of our neighbors here,Wei Hong, was once nominated asFilial and Incorruptby the local authorities; he is willing to share his wealth to support a righteous cause, and his family is very rich. With his help, we might hope for success.”

Cao Caoset out wine and prepared a feast, then invitedWei Hongto their home.Cao Caosaid to him, “The house ofHanno longer has any true leader, whileDong Zhuois monopolizing power: he coerces the sovereign and terrorizes the people, and all the realm gnash their teeth with rage. I would like to exert myself to bracing up the altars of the state, regretting only that I lack the power to do so. As you are a loyal and righteous gentleman, sir, I presume to ask you to aid me.”

Wei Hongreplied, “I have long had the same desire; it was just that I had not yet found some hero fit to undertake the task. Since you have such a grand ambition,Mengde, I willingly devote all my property to the cause.”

Cao Caowas overjoyed. He first drafted a false edict calling upon the realm to rise up and had it spread around to every corner, then began bringing in soldiers for the cause, assembling his new troops under a white banner with “Loyalty and Righteousness” printed upon it. In just a few days, so many gentlemen were answering his call that they flowed in like sheets of rain.

One day, two men came to join his cause: one was a man fromWei countyinYangping commandery,Yue Jin, styledWenqian, while the other was a native ofJuye countyinShanyang commandery,Li Dian, styledMancheng.Cao Caokept both of them on his personal staff. Then there was a man fromQiao countyin thePeiprincedom:Xiahou Dun, styledYuanrang, who was a descendant ofXiahou Ying. ThisXiahou Dunhad trained in the spear and the club since his youth. At the age of thirteen, he learned combat under a master, and when one day someone had spoken disrespectfully of his master,Xiahou Dunkilled the man and went into hiding. At this time, hearing thatCao Caowas raising troops, he came to offer his services, accompanied by his younger kinsmanXiahou Yuan. Each brought with them a thousand strongmen.12•••

The novel sometimes does these mass recruitments with a lot of names thrown in, you don’t need to try to memorize them though some will be major figures.↩

Xiahou Yingwas a founding Minister and senoir general of the Han dynasty.↩

Such mass recruitments show the ability of a great leader to draw worthy people to themselves and recognize their talents immediately.

In fact, these two men were like brothers toCao Cao, for his fatherCao Songwas originally a scion of the Xiahou clan, only having been adopted into the Cao clan later. Hence, they were all blood relatives.

A few days later came two cousins,Cao Ren, styledZixiao, andCao Hong, styledZilian, each of whom brought more than a thousand additional soldiers. These two were accomplished horsemen and trained in the use of arms.Cao Caowas delighted, and set them to drilling and training the soldiers in the village.Wei Hongspent his family treasure freely in procuring clothing, armor, flags and banners, while countless gifts of grain poured in from all sides.

At the time,Yuan Shaohad received the forged edictCao Caomade. He assembled his civil and military officials, and led an army of thirty thousand to march fromBohaito joinCao Cao’s coalition.Cao Caothen drew up a proclamation to distribute to the local commanderies. He wrote:

In the spirit of great righteousness, we signatories,Cao Caoand the rest, hereby make the following declaration to the realm.

Dong Zhuohas cheated Heaven and deceived Earth by wrecking the state and murdering the sovereign, and he is bringing filth and disorder to the inner palace, and bringing injury and oppression to both the living and the dead. He is vicious and cruel without benevolence, and his crimes and offenses are heaped up! In accordance with a secret edict from theSon of Heaven, we have called up soldiers and have pledged to cleanse the heartland and destroy the villains. May each of you raise righteous troops as well and join in the public indignation, in order to support the royal family and rescue the people from misery. On the day you receive this declaration, may you act at once!

OnceCao Cao’s declaration had gone out, many of the regional garrison commanders and lords raised their troops to support the cause as well. They were:

TheGeneral of the RearandGrand AdministratorofNanyang,Yuan Shu.

TheInspectorofJi province,Han Fu.

TheInspectorofYu province,Kong Zhou.

TheInspectorofYan province,Liu Dai.

TheGrand AdministratorofHenei,Wang Kuang.

TheGrand AdministratorofChenliu,Zhang Miao.

TheGrand AdministratorofDong,Qiao Mao.

TheGrand AdministratorofShanyang,Yuan Yi.

TheChancellorofJibei,Bao Xin.

TheGrand AdministratorofBeihai,Kong Rong.

TheGrand AdministratorofGuangling,Zhang Chao.

TheInspectorofXu province,Tao Qian.

TheGrand AdministratorofLiang province,Ma Teng.

TheGrand AdministratorofBeiping,Gongsun Zan.

TheGrand AdministratorofShangdang,Zhang Yang.

TheMarquisofWuchengandGrand AdministratorofChangsha,Sun Jian.

TheTownship MarquisofQixiangandGrand AdministratorofBohai,Yuan Shao.

These contingents varied in strength—some having thirty thousand troops while others had only ten or twenty thousand—but each was organized with a contingent of civil and military officers. They headed towardLuoyang, the capital.

It should be mentioned that whileGongsun Zan, theGrand AdministratorofBeiping, was on his way to join the others at the head of fifteen thousand veteran troops, he passed throughPingyuaninDezhou. Along the way, in a distant grove of mulberry trees, he spotted several riders under a yellow banner riding toward him. As they drew near he recognized their leader as an old classmate,Liu Xuande.

“Good brother, what are you doing here?” askedGongsun Zan.

Xuandereplied, “It was thanks to your past kindness that I have been appointed as magistrate ofPingyuan. When I heard you were leading your great army through, I came out to have a look. Elder brother, I pray you enter the city to water your horses.”

Gongsun Zan, pointing toGuan YuandZhang Fei, asked, “And who might these two be?”

“These areGuan YuandZhang Fei, my sworn brothers,” saidXuande.

“They helped you in fighting theYellow Turbans?”

“All my success was due to their efforts,” saidXuande.

“And what offices do they fill?” askedGongsun Zan.

“Guan Yuis amounted archer,” answeredXuande. “Zhang Feiis afoot archer.”

“Thus are able men buried!” sighedGongsun Zan. “All the lords of the realm are now going toLuoyangto destroy the traitorousDong Zhuo. My brother, why not abandon this paltry office, and join us in campaign to support theHouse of Han?”

“I should like to go,” saidXuande.

“If you had let me kill that traitor earlier, we would not be having trouble today,” quippedZhang Fei.•••

Murdering a senior general might have prevented a future coup, but would perhaps not have ended well forLiu Beiand company.

“The past is in the past,” saidYunchang. “Let’s get our things together and head out.”

Thus the three brothers, along with a few horsemen, joinedGongsun Zanand marched with him to join the great army.

One after another the feudal lords arrived and encamped. Their camps extending over two hundred li in all. Once everyone had arrived,Cao Caomade offerings of sacrificial bullocks and horses, and called all the lords to a great assembly to decide upon a plan of attack.

Wang Kuang,Grand AdministratorofHenei, spoke, “Since we have all come here to support a great cause, we must first appoint a leader for our coalition. Only once the army has its commander, and pledged obedience, can we advance.”

Cao Caoproposed, “Yuan Benchushould lead us. His family has wielded power at the highest levels of government for four generations, and their former officials and allies are everywhere. As a descendant of renowned ministers of theHan, no other is better suited to take command.”

Yuan Shaoagain and again declined this honor. But the others all said, “No one butBenchuwill do,” and in the end he agreed toserve.3•••

As well asYuan Shao’s role against the eunuchs and early opposition toDong Zhuo, the Yuan’s were rivaled only by one (outside the imperial clan) as the most powerful, and wealthiest, family of the Han.↩

The next day, a three-storied altar was built, and they planted about it five banners—one for each garrison—in five directions. Above it they set up white yaks’ tails, golden axes, and seals andtalliesof military authority.

All being ready,Yuan Shaowas invited to ascend the altar. Clad in ceremonial robes and girt with a sword,Yuan Shaoascended reverently. There he burned incense, twice made obeisance, and recited this oath:

“TheHouse of Hanhas fallen upon evil days, the bands of imperial authority are loosened. The rebel ministerDong Zhuotakes advantage of the discord to work evil, and calamity falls even upon the most exalted while cruelty overwhelms simple folk. We,Yuan Shaoand his confederates, fearing for the safety of the imperial altars, have assembled military forces to rush to the needs of the state. We now pledge ourselves to exert our whole strength and to act in concord to the utmost limit of our powers. There must be no disconcerted or selfish action. Should any depart from this pledge, may he lose his life and leave no posterity.Almighty HeavenandUniversal Mother Earthand the enlightened spirits of our forefathers, be ye our witnesses!”

The reading finished, he smeared the blood of the sacrifice upon his lips and upon the lips of those who shared the pledge. All were deeply affected by the gravity of his words and spirit, and many shed tears.

With the ceremony concluded,Yuan Shaodescended and was led to his tent, where he took the place of honor. The other lords arranged themselves into two rows according to rank and age.Cao Caopassed around the wine.

“Now that we have selected our leader,” saidCao Cao, “each of us must listen to whatever he commands for the sake of working together, and to support the state. There must be no rivalry or jostling for power based on the size of our own forces.”

AndYuan Shaodeclared, “Unworthy as I am, you have elected me your chief, and I must reward merit and punish offenses impartially. Let each see to it that he obeys the national laws and the army precepts. These must not be broken.”

“We shall hear and obey!” cried all.

“My brother,Yuan Shu, is appointedChief of the Commissariat. He must see to it that the whole camp is well supplied and no one goes without. But the need of the moment for a vanguard leader, who shall go toSi River Passand provoke a battle. The other forces must take up positions in support.”

Sun Jian,Grand AdministratorofChangshaspoke, “I am willing to lead the van.”

“Wentaiis valiant and fierce, equal to this service,” agreedYuan Shao.4•••

We sawSun Jianbriefly in chapter 2 helping fight the Turban remnants and then rebellions in the south.↩

Sun Jianthus led out his own troops and began making his way towardsSi River Pass. The garrison there sent a swift rider back to theAssisting Chancellor’s office atthe capitalto report the emergency.

Ever sinceDong Zhuohad secured his position, he had given himself over to daily luxury. When news of the emergency reached the adviserLi Ru, he at once brought word to his master. Much alarmed,Dong Zhuocalled a great council.

Lü Bu, the Marquis ofWen, rose to speak, “Do not fear, my father. I look upon these lords beyond the passes as so many blades of grass. Let me bring our army of tigers and wolves to take their heads and hang them from the capital gates.”

Dong Zhuowas delighted. “As long as I haveFengxian, I can sleep secure!”

Yet before he finished speaking, someone behindLü Buexclaimed, “Wouldn’t that be a case of ‘a ox-cleaver to kill a chicken’? The Marquis need not trouble himself: I will claim their heads as easily as if removing items from a sack.”

Dong Zhuolooked over and saw a man of exceptional height, lithe like a tiger, supple like a wolf, with a round head like a leopard and apelike arms. He wasHua Xiong, a native ofGuanxi region.Dong Zhuorejoiced atHua Xiong’s bold words, appointed himColonel of Agile Cavalryand assigned him fifty thousand horse and foot.Hua Xiongand three others—Li Su,Hu Zhen, andZhao Cen—quickly marched out towardSi River Pass.

Among the feudal lords,Bao Xinwas jealous thatSun Jian, as the vanguard leader, might earn the highest honors. He secretly dispatched his younger brother,Bao Zhong, with three thousand soldiers to reachSi River Passfirst by way of side paths to avoid attention. WhenBao Zhongarrived with his small force, he offered battle.Hua Xiong, at the head of five hundred mail-clad horsemen, swept down from thepass, calling out, “Stand and fight, rebel!”

Bao Zhongswiftly attempted to retreat, yetHua Xionglifted his blade and hacked down, andBao Zhong’s head fell from his horse. Most of his company were captured.Hua XiongsentBao Zhong’s head back to theAssisting Chancellor’s office along with a report of his victory, andDong Zhuofurther appointed him as Commander.•••

Not a great start for the coalition, for the ill-discipline and broken vows as much as the defeat.

Sun Jianpresently approached thepasshimself. He had four captains:Cheng Pu, styledDemou, a native ofTuyininBeiping, who fought with an steel-spined spear with a serpent-headed blade;Huang Gai, styledGongfu, a native ofLingling, who wielded an iron whip;Han Dang, styledYigong, a broad-swordsman fromLingzhiinLiaoxi; andZu Mao, styledDarong, a man fromFuchuninWu, who fought with a pair ofblades.5•••

As one of the Han’s most experienced generals at this time,Sun Jianhad collected a core of men around him. Like with theCao Caoinflux, don’t worry about memorizing all the names.↩

Sun Jianwore armor of fine silver and a crimson turban. Belted across his body was his ancient ingot iron sword, and he rode upon a dappled horse with a flowing mane.

Pointing up at those atop thePass,Sun Jianyelled out, “You slaves of a villain, why don’t you surrender already?”Hu Zhenled five thousand soldiers out to meet the enemy in battle.Cheng Puwith the serpent lance rode out and went straight forHu Zhen, and in just a few bouts he put his lance throughHu Zhen’s throat and left him dead on the ground. ThenSun Jiansignaled the rest of his troops to advance. But from thePassthey rained down showers of stones, which proved too much for the assailants.Sun Jianhad his soldiers pull back to camp atLiangdong, while sending a messenger to informYuan Shaoof his initial success and to request an urgent supply shipment fromYuan Shu.

Someone advisedYuan Shu, “ThisSun Jianis a fierce tiger from theSouthlands; if he were to takethe capitaland destroyDong Zhuo, we will only have exchanged a wolf for a tiger. Do not send him any grain. Starve his troops, and his army is sure toscatter.”•••

Another setback for team unity

AndYuan Shuheeded this detractor and sent no grain. SoonSun Jian’s hungry soldiers began to grumble and get rowdy, and enemy spies brought this news to the defenders of thePass.Li Suthen advisedHua Xiong, “Tonight I’ll take some troops out along a back road to launch a surprise attack againstSun Jian’s camp. If you sweep down upon him from the front, General, we can captureSun Jian.”

Hua Xiongagreed, ordering his soldiers to eat a full dinner and to head out from thePassthat night; the moon was bright and the wind was still, and by the time they reachedSun Jian’s camp it was midnight. Then they beat the drums and charged into the camp.Sun Jianhastily donned his fighting gear and rode out. He ran straight intoHua Xiongand the two warriors engaged, yet before they had exchanged many passes,Li Subrought up his men from the other side and began ordering his soldiers to set fires all around.

Sun Jian’s army was thrown into confusion and fled like rats. A general melee ensued and soon onlyZu Maowas left at his chief’s side. These two broke through the encirclement and fled.Hua Xiongcoming in hot pursuit,Sun Jiantook his magpie-painted bow and let fly two arrows in quick succession, butHua Xiongdodged both of them, and whenSun Jianfitted a third arrow to the string, he drew his arm back so fiercely that the bow snapped. He cast the bow to the earth and set off at full gallop.

Zu Maotold him, “My lord, your crimson turban is a mark that the rebels will too easily recognize. Give it to me and I will wear it.”

SoSun Jianexchanged his silver helmet with the turban for his faithful friend’s headpiece, and the two men parted, riding different ways. Seeking only to catch the man in the crimson turban,Hua Xiong’s troops chased afterZu Mao, whileSun Jianslipped away along a byroad.

Zu Mao, hotly pursued, then tore off the headdress which he hung on the post of a half-burned house as he passed and dashed into the thick woods.Hua Xiong’s men spotted the crimson turban and surrounded it but did not dare to approach. Only after shooting some arrows at it did they discover the trick, and they went up to claim the helmet.

This was the momentZu Maohad been waiting for. He burst forth from the woods, rushing forth with swords whirling about, and made forHua Xiong. ButHua Xiongwas too quick, and with a fierce cry he slashed atZu Maodown from his horse with a single swordstroke. The slaughter atSun Jian’s camp continued until sunrise.Hua Xiongthen led his men back to thepass.

Cheng Pu,Huang Gai, andHan Dangsoon reunited withSun Jian, and they all led their remaining soldiers to camp at some distance fromSi River Pass.Sun Jianwas much grieved at the loss of his generous subordinateZu Mao.

Sun Jiannow sent another messenger through the night to informYuan Shaoof what had happened; greatly astonished,Yuan Shaoexclaimed, “To think thatSun Wentaiwould be defeated at the hands ofHua Xiong!”

Yuan Shaopromptly called all the lords to a council. At this timeGongsun Zanarrived with his own forces, andYuan Shaoinvited him into the tent to take his place alongside the other lords. When all were seated,Yuan Shaosaid, “The other day, GeneralBao Xin’s younger brother disobeying the rules we made for our orderly advance, and attacked the enemy rashly. He was slain, along with many of our soldiers. NowSun Wentaihas been defeated byHua Xiong. Our fighting spirit has suffered. What is to bedone?”•••

Things can surely only get better?

None of the lords said anything. Yet asYuan Shaoraised his eyes to look around the tent, he noticed that standing behindGongsun Zanwere three men of striking appearance, all smiling grimly.

“AdministratorGongsun Zan, who are these men behind you?” askedYuan Shao.

Gongsun ZantoldXuandeto step forward. “This is my brother and the classmate of my youth,” said he, “thePrefectofPingyuan,Liu Bei.”

“Might he be the sameLiu Xuandewho helped break theYellow Turbans?” remarkedCao Cao.

“The very same,” saidGongsun Zan, and he orderedXuandeto make his obeisance to the assembly as he relatedXuande’s services and his origin, all in full detail.

“A man of theHanimperial lineage ought to have his own seat,” saidYuan Shao, commanding that one be brought out for him.

Liu Beimodestly attempted to decline.

SaidYuan Shao, “This consideration is not for your fame and office; I respect you as a scion of the imperial family.”

SoLiu Beitook his seat in the lowest place of the long line of lords, while his two brothers with folded arms took their stations behindhim.•••

Three attitudes on show.Liu Beihumble,Cao CaonotingLiu Bei’s military successes, whileYuan Shaocares only for the lineage.

Suddenly a scout came in to report, “Hua Xionghas led his mail-clad cavalry down from thePass, waving about AdministratorSun Jian’s crimson turban atop a long pole. They are assembled in front of the camp to taunt us and call for battle.”

“Who dares go out to give battle?” saidYuan Shao.

Yu She, a general of renown underYuan Shu, stepped forward. “I am willing to go.”

Yuan Shaowas pleased and ordered him to ride out. Yet almost immediately someone came back to report, “Yu Shedid not even last three bouts againstHua XiongbeforeHua Xiongbeheaded him.”

Fear began to lay its cold hand on the assembly. ThenHan Fusaid, “I have a brave warrior among my men.Pan Fengis his name, and he could slay thisHua Xiong.”

Yuan ShaoorderedPan Fengto go. With his great battle-ax in hand,Pan Fengmounted and rode forth. But soon came another report: “Pan Fenghas likewise been beheaded byHua Xiong.” The assembly turnedpale.•••

If this is whatHua Xiongcan do, how much mightier isLu Bu?

“What a pity my two able leaders,Yan LiangandWen Chou, are not here yet!” sighedYuan Shao. “If only one were here, why would we have to fearHua Xiong?”•••

Turning up for such a major campaign without your star generals feels a little weird.

At those words a voice from the back of the assembly boomed out, “I offer to takeHua Xiong’s head and lay it before you personally!”

The assembly turned to see the speaker, who stood at the tent entrance. He was over nine spans tall and had a long beard. His eyes gleamed like a phoenix and his eyebrows were thick and bushy as caterpillars; his face was a swarthy red, and his voice was deep as the sound of a great bell.

“Who is this man?” askedYuan Shao.

Gongsun Zantold them, “This isLiu Xuande’s younger brother,Guan Yu.”

“And what is his position?”

“He servesLiu Xuandeas amounted archer.”

“You’re mocking us!” roared outYuan Shufrom his place. “Who is somearcherlike you to speak so brashly to lords like us, as though we lacked for generals? Let us drive him out of here!”

ButCao Caointervened. “Calm yourself,Gonglu! If he is willing to talk so big, he must be brave and cunning. We can at least let him try; it will not be too late to reproach him if he fails.”

“Hua Xiongwill laugh at us if we send a commonarcherto fight him,” saidYuan Shao.

“He hardly seems a common fellow. And how can the enemy know he is but a bowman?” saidCao Cao.

AndLord Guanpledged, “If I fail, I ask you to cut off my head.”

Cao Caohad someone pour out a cup of heated wine, thatLord Guanmight drink it as he rodeout.•••

There is quite the contrast betweenCao Cao, willing to try this brave man, and the Yuan’s who both worry about how it will look.

“Just set the cup aside for me,” saidLord Guan. “I’ll be back soon enough.”

He left the tent blade in hand and vaulted into the saddle. The lords in the tent heard the fierce roll of the drums from beyond the gates of the camp, and then a mighty sound as if skies were falling and earth rising, hills trembling and mountains tearing asunder. And they were sore afraid. Just as they were about to send someone to see what was happening, they heard the gentle tinkle of horse bells as a horse entered the camp: it wasYunchang, holding the head ofHua Xiong, which he tossed to the ground.

His wine was still warm!

This doughty deed has been celebrated in verse.

His power shook Heaven and Earth—the foremost deed.

War drums rolled at the gate before the camp.

ThenYunchangset down his cup to display his valor—

And the wine was still warm whenHua Xionglay dead.

Cao Caowas delighted at this success.

But thenZhang Feisuddenly dashed out from behindXuande, shouting, “Now that my brother has takenHua Xiong’s head, what are we waiting for? Let’s break through thepassright now and takeDong Zhuoalive!”

Yuan Shufumed, “Even a great minister like me knows his place! Why do we suffer this petty magistrate and his pet brutes to flaunt their strength and vent their spleen? We ought to banish them all from the tent!”

Cao Caosaid, “A job well done deserves its reward. What does a man’s status have to do with it?”

“If you all think this county magistrate is so invaluable,”Yuan Shuseethed, “perhaps it is I who should take my leave.”

Cao Caosighed to himself, “Can a single stray remark really frustrate so great an endeavor?”

But he indeed commandedGongsun Zanto takeXuande,Guan Yu, andZhang Feiback to his own camp, although once the other chiefs had each dispersed as well,Cao Caosecretly sent presents of beef and wine to console and reassure the three brothers.

Meanwhile, the survivors ofHua Xiong’s troops had returned toSi River Passand recounted their defeat; much alarmed,Li Suhad hastily drafted an urgent report and sent word back toDong Zhuo.Dong Zhuoat once summonedLi Ru,Lü Bu, and his other trusted advisers to a council.

Li Ruargued, “We have lost a great leader inHua Xiongwhile the rebel power has swelled immensely. And I must point out that the leader of this confederacy isYuan Shao, whose uncleYuan Weiis still serving as theGrand Tutor; if we put ourselves in a position where uncle and nephew can strike together from without and within, we shall suffer greatly. We must make the first move.Assisting Chancellor, I ask that you take the lead of our forces yourself and direct our forces to root out these villains.”

Dong Zhuoagreed. He summonedLi JueandGuo Siand sent them with five hundred soldiers to surround the home ofYuan Wei, where they executed everyone regardless of age;Yuan Wei’s head was hung up out in front of the gates as a warning.

In the meantime,Dong Zhuohad raised an army of two hundred thousand, and he sent it marching out along two roads:Li JueandGuo Siled one army of fifty thousand out to holdSi River Pass, with orders not to fight any rash battle, whileDong Zhuohimself tookLi Ru,Lü Bu,Fan Chou,Zhang Ji, and his other officers with the remaining hundred and fifty thousand soldiers to defendTiger Trap Pass, fifty li out fromLuo.

Upon arriving atTiger Trap Pass,Dong ZhuoorderedLü Buto take thirty thousand soldiers and make a strong stockade in front of of thePass, while the main body withDong Zhuooccupied thePassitself.

Scout riders brought news of these movements during the night, and once it was brought to his attentionYuan Shaosummoned a new council of the lords.Cao Caoargued, “By garrisoning troops at Hulao,Dong Zhuois going to cut off our advance. Let’s send half of our troops to confront him.”

Yuan Shaothus ordered eight of the lords to march toTiger Trap Passto deal with this new threat:Wang Kuang,Qiao Mao,Bao Xin,Yuan Yi,Kong Rong,Zhang Yang,Tao Qian, andGongsun Zan.Cao Caoled his own troops to follow behind them to act as areserve.•••

紹乃分王匡，喬瑁，鮑信，袁遺，孔融，張楊，陶謙，公孫瓚，八路諸侯，往虎牢關迎敵。操引軍往來救應。

These eight lords set out one by one, withWang Kuang’s troops the first to reachTiger Trap Pass.Lü Bubrought three thousandiron cavalryto dash forward to face them. OnceWang Kuanghad ordered his army, horse and foot, in battle array, he took his station under the great banner and looked over at his foe.

He watched asLü Buemerged from the opposing ranks. On his head was a three-pronged headdress of ruddy gold. For an inner garment he wore a robe ofXichuan silkembroidered with flowers, and over that his breast and back were covered by mail adorned with a gaping animal’s head, joined by rings at the sides and girt to his waist with a belt fastened by a beautiful lion-head clasp. His bow and arrows were slung on his shoulders and he carried atrident-halberd. He was seated on his snorting steed Red Hare. Truly he was the man among men, as his steed the horse among horses!

Wang Kuangturned his head to ask, “Who dares go out to fight him?” A general rode out from behind, with galloping horse and steady spear;Wang Kuangrecognized him as a famous officer from Henei,Fang Yue. The two champions clashed, yet before the fifth boutFang Yuefell under a thrust of the halberd. ThenLü Bugripped his halberd firmly and led his cavalry to charge towards them:Wang Kuang’s army was greatly defeated and his soldiers began scattering in all directions, whileLü Budashed this way and that with such ease it was as if he had the field all to himself.

Luckily forWang Kuang, it was at this time thatQiao MaoandYuan Yicame up with their own troops: they came to his rescue, andLü Buat last fell back. The three lords having each lost many men, they withdrew thirty li and made a stockade. Soon the remaining five lords arrived as well, and they came together in council.

“A bold hero, thisLü Bu,” they said to one another. “No one is a match for him.”

Even as they worried about the man, a scout came in to report, “Lü Buis offering battle.” Thus they each mounted their horses and arrayed their eight armies spread across heights and hills. In the distance they sawLü Bu’s troops as well, their embroidered banners waving in the breeze, asLü Burode out in front of their lines.

One ofZhang Yang’s generals,Mu Shun, rode out with his lance at the ready, butLü Bulifted his halberd and slew him with a single thrust, greatly unnerving the soldiers of the lords. ThenKong Rong’s generalWu Anguogalloped out as well, wielding an iron mace;Lü Buwhirling his halberd and urging on his steed came to meet him. The two fought, well matched for a dozen bouts, when a blow from the halberd brokeWu Anguo’s wrist, causing him to drop the mace to the ground and flee. The soldiers of the eight lords marched up to save him, andLü Buonce again fell back.

The lords withdrew to their own camp as well, then held another council.

Cao Caostarted to say, “None of us can match the prowess of thisLü Bu. We had better assemble all of the lords and come up with some kind of better strategy together. If onlyLü Buwere taken,Dong Zhuocould easily bekilled.”•••

曹操曰：「呂布英勇無敵，可會十八路諸侯，共議良策。若擒了呂布，董卓易誅耳。」

Yet once again, even as they were still discussing the matter they were informed thatLü Buhad brought out his troops to offer battle. The eight lords thus led their own troops out again as well.

This timeGongsun Zanhimself, flourishing his spear, went to face the enemy. After just a few bouts,Gongsun Zansaw that he was losing and turned to flee. YetLü Buinsisted on giving chase, and how couldGongsun Zanhope to outride Red Hare, the sort of horse that could run a thousand li in a day and who galloped as swift as the wind?

As the coalition soldiers watched, Red Hare gained rapidly upon the flying horseman and his rider’s halberd was just about to skewerGongsun Zanthrough the heart. But suddenly a third rider rode up, with round glaring eyes and a bristling mustache and armed with a long snake-like spear.

“Stop right there, you double bastard!” the man roared. “Zhang FeiofYanishere!”•••

飛馬大叫：「三姓家奴休走！燕人張飛在此！」

Seeing this opponent,Lü BuletGongsun Zanbe to engage this new adversary. Roused by his vigor and spirit,Zhang Feiproved a worthy foe forLü Bu: they exchanged half a hundred bouts with no advantage to either side. Seeing this stalemate,Guan Yugave his horse a slap, rode out with his huge and weightyGreen Dragon, and attackedLü Bufrom the other flank. Yet even while having to battle a foe to either side, another thirty bouts passed andLü Bustill stood firm. ThenLiu Xuandetoo rode out on a yellow-maned horse, his twin swords raised ready to strike, and took up another angle to assist his brothers; the trio circled aroundLü Bu, and the flashing of weapon against weapon was like the flickers of spinning lanterns. The soldiers of the eight lords all stood transfixed at the sight.

Lü Bucould tell that his reflexes were slipping. Thus he looked meaningfully atXuande’s face and feinted with a thrust of his halberd:Xuandeflinched to evade the expected blow, andLü Buexploited the opening to escape from the encirclement and then galloped away, hanging his halberd behindhim.•••

呂布架隔遮攔不定，看著玄德面上，虛刺一戟，玄德急閃。呂布蕩開陣角，倒拖畫戟，飛馬便回。

But was it likely they would allow him to escape? They whipped their steeds and followed hard. The men of the eight armies cracked their throats with thunderous cheers and all dashed forward, pressing afterLü Buas he made for the shelter of thePass. And first among his pursuers were the three heroicbrothers.•••

三個那裏肯捨，拍馬趕來。八路軍兵，喊聲大震，一齊掩殺。呂布軍馬，望關上奔走；玄德、關、張隨後趕來。

An ancient poet has told of this famous fight in these lines:

The fateful day ofHancame in the reigns of Huan and Ling,

Their glory declined as the sun sinks at the close of day.

Dong Zhuo, infamous minister, pulled the young sovereign from throne.

It is trueLiu Xiewas a weakling, too timid for his times.

ThenCao Caoproclaimed abroad these wicked deeds,

And the great lords, moved with anger, assembled their forces.

In council met they and chose as their oath-chiefYuan Shao,

Pledged themselves to maintain the ruling house and tranquility.

Of the warriors of that time peerlessLü Buwas the boldest.

His valor and prowess are sung by all within the four seas.

He clothed his body in silver armor like the scales of a dragon,

On his head was a golden headdress, fastened with a massive pin,

About his waist a belt, the clasp, two wild beasts’ heads with gripping jaws,

His flowing, broidered robe fluttered about his form,

His swift courser bounded over the plain, a mighty wind following,

His terrible halberd flashed in the sunlight, bright as a placid lake.

Who dared face him as he rode forth to challenge?

The bowels of the lords were torn with fear and hearts trembled within.

Then leaped forthZhang Fei, the valiant warrior of the north,

Gripped in his mighty hand the long snakelike spear,

His mustache bristled with anger, standing stiff like wire.

His round eyes glared, lightning flashes darted from them.

Neither quailed in the fight, but the issue was undecided.

Guan Yunchangstood out in front, his soul vexed within him,

Green Dragonhis sword shone white as hoar frost in the sunlight,

His bright colored fighting robe fluttered like butterfly wings,

Demons and angels shrieked at the thunder of his horse hoofs,

In his eyes was fierce anger, a fire to be quenched only in blood.

NextXuandejoined the battle, gripping his twin sword blades,

The heavens themselves trembled at the majesty of his wrath.

These three closely besetLü Buand long drawn out was the battle,

Always he warded their blows, never faltering a moment.

The noise of their shouting rose to the sky, and the earth reechoed it,

The heat of battle ranged to the frozen pole star.

Worn out, feeling his strength fast ebbing,Lü Buthought to flee,

He glanced at the hills around and thither would fly for shelter,

Then, reversing his halberd and lowering its lofty point,

Hastily he fled, loosing himself from the battle;

With head low bent, he gave the rein to his courser,

Turned his face away and fled toTiger Trap Pass.

The three brothers maintained the pursuit toTiger Trap Pass. Looking up, they saw an immense canopy of black gauze atop thePass, fluttering in the west wind.

“That has to beDong Zhuo,” exclaimedZhang Fei. “What are we chasingLü Buso hard for? Let’s get that banditDong Zhuofirst and cut the whole thing out by the roots!”

He whipped up his steed towardTiger Trap Passto captureDong Zhuo.

And so it was:

To quell rebellion seize the leader if you can;

If you need a wondrous service then first find a wondrous man.

Who proved the battle’s victor? The next chapter will reveal.
